title: ICIS OUTLOOK: What to expect from European energy markets in H2 2026: 10 key questions
summary: The first half of 2026 was defined by the return of geopolitical risk to European energy markets. Expectations of a comfortable year were upended by the outbreak of war in Iran and the disruption to L...
content:
The first half of 2026 was defined by the return of geopolitical risk to European energy markets. Expectations of a comfortable year were upended by the outbreak of war in Iran and the disruption to LNG flows through the Strait of Hormuz, driving higher gas prices and renewed concerns over winter supply security.

Power markets largely followed gas, while carbon markets saw heightened volatility amid concerns over potential political intervention and the future direction of EU climate policy.

We asked ICIS experts 10 key questions on what market participants should expect in the second half of the year for European gas, power and carbon markets.

When do you expect the Strait of Hormuz to re-open?

Andreas Schroeder (Head of Gas Analytics): Our current base case view is a return of LNG output from Qatar and United Arab Emirates by August 2026 at a reduced capacity due to the damage to two of the fourteen LNG trains at Ras Laffan. We do forecast some limited output of less than 10% of capacity in the near-term, mainly due to deliveries to Kuwait inside the Strait of Hormuz.

While a full reopening by August is still possible, it also is relatively optimistic given the current ongoing stalemate in peace talks. A closure of the strait for much longer than August would obviously have bullish implications for the gas market. We therefore run a regular scenario with a one-year long Hormuz closure out to March 2027.

How quickly do LNG flows normalise once Hormuz reopens?

Andreas Schroeder: Qatar’s Ras Laffan plant continues to produce at a low rate below 10%. A full restart of twelve trains is likely to take weeks, given the number of steps and the risk of an unexpected issue during the process. Empty vessels would need multiple days, if not weeks, to return to Qatar with some already under sub-charter.

Once loaded, we expect journey times to Europe of around four weeks around the Cape of Good Hope, as shippers avoid the shorter 18-day-route through the Suez Canal. Altogether, I expect LNG arrivals in Europe would take some two months after restart.

Could we expect gas prices to decline as soon as the Strait re-opens? How much more upside risk is there to continued closure?

Andreas Schroeder: A sudden end to the Iran conflict would lead to a prompt drop in prices towards the mid-30 to 40 €/MWh range, just slightly above pre-crisis levels. Our base case with the Strait reopening by August sees gas prices higher than current market levels.

Europe needs to increase its prices to compete more for LNG against Asia if it wants to refill its storage ahead of winter. Our scenario with a prolonged blockade of Hormuz is yet more bullish with prices rising closer to 55-60 €/MWh range.

Will Europe reach its gas storage targets ahead of winter?

Andreas Schroeder: European market participants have delayed injections into storage and we expect more during late summer. Stocks started the year quite low and current levels are below 2025, which followed a cold winter, and are a long way below the mild years of 2023/2024. They are more in line with 2022, the year the Russia-Ukraine war began.

It is still possible to refill storage, but the trajectory of injections would need to increase. If we keep the slow rate since mid-April, storage would only reach less than 80 Bcm (73%) by November, 20 Bcm (18 percentage points) lower than other recent years.

To make up that gap to hit 90% fullness, Europe needs to inject 4 Bcm more per month until October, which is around 130 mcm/day or 1.3 more LNG cargoes per day. This is achievable but needs Europe to speed up injections and to incentivise the LNG to flow. In our base case, we expect gas storage to achieve the lower tolerance threshold of 81% filling by early November.

Markets are currently not incentivising storage injections. Seasonal price spreads are deeply negative. Time spreads need to move more positive to make storage work whilst preserving incentives to attract global LNG to Europe during summer. Given the challenging market environment, governments are increasingly interfering into markets. Most importantly, the Netherlands enforces state-backed refilling of their sites over this summer irrespective of cost.

Does Europe have much more potential for fuel switch if prices rise?

Matthew Jones (Head of Power Analytics): Most of the fuel switch in Europe has already happened due to price movements since late February. If we compare the expectations at current price levels to those on the eve of the Iran war, we can already expect around a 9TWh drop per month in gas-fired generation across Europe for the rest of the year.

However, there is still some fuel switch potential left in Europe. If gas prices were to rise by 20%, we could expect European gas-fired generation for the rest of the year to fall by another 3TWh per month (-9%) compared to current expectations.

Can we expect a significant decline in power and gas demand from the Iran war?

Matthew Jones: Power demand has increased by 1.5% year on year in the first five months of the year. This may seem surprising given the context of the Iran war, but Europe is not facing anything akin to the Ukraine crisis in 2022. Over the past 3 months, TTF prices have averaged €48/MWh, while Eurozone inflation has averaged 2.9%. The equivalent figures in the three months following the start of the Ukraine war were €108/MWh and 7.7%.

In addition, the Ukraine war led to a European energy crisis, whereas the Iran war has created a global energy crisis. This is important for much of industry as they care about relative economics vs competitors as well as absolute economics. With Asia’s prices having increased even more than Europe’s, many industrial producers are not facing as much as a competitive challenge as they were back in 2022.

If the Strait of Hormuz opens over the summer, I would expect very minimal impact on gas and power demand at the annual level. A longer closure could be expected to lead to some demand destruction, but still at very modest levels.

What impact will the upcoming EU ETS reform (15 July) have on the EUA market balance?

Lewis Unstead (Senior Carbon Analyst): The key focus for the market will be the “Investment Booster”, which involves auctioning 400Mt of EUAs to raise ~€30bn for industrial decarbonisation. In our view, this represents the single biggest mid-term sensitivity for the EUA balance. However, we think legislation is unlikely to be fully ratified before H2 2027, meaning additional auctions would only begin from late-2027 onwards. As a result, any bearish impact is more likely to be concentrated in the 2028-29 balance, where our modelling shows a high probability of the market shifting into oversupply.

That leaves the EUA market in an interesting position: near-term fundamentals for the 2026 compliance cycle still look structurally tight, while the market simultaneously must contend with a growing risk of structurally loose balances in 2028-29. We think the market will be reluctant to fully price-in the bearish impact of the latter in second half of this year.

Importantly, the Commission’s proposal on 15 July is only the starting point of the legislative process, with Parliament and Council ultimately determining what gets adopted. The aim is for co‑legislators to reach political agreement by end‑Q1 2027, although it remains unclear whether some elements could be separated from the wider package in order to fast‑track negotiations.

How will the Commission respond to Member State concerns about excessively high carbon price?

Lewis Unstead: Member States have called on the Commission to reduce volatility of the carbon price, but so far no measures have been taken to directly address these concerns. We expect this to be key area of focus in the upcoming EU ETS review.

Under the current ETS framework, Article 29a is designed to address excessive price increases. It allows for the release of 75m allowances from the MSR if prices remain 2.4 times above the two-year average for six consecutive months. In practice, this threshold is far too high to be triggered under current market conditions (prices need to average €170/t over next six months to trigger) making the mechanism effectively redundant.

As part of the ETS review, we expect this safeguard to be recalibrated to respond more quickly to price spikes. This would likely involve lowering the trigger threshold and shortening the observation period. Policymakers may use ETS2 as a blueprint, where MSR

injections are triggered if prices exceed 1.5 times the previous six-month average over a three-month period, making it much more responsive.

Ultimately, any proposal to adjust Article 29a will be closely watched as a signal of what carbon price levels policymakers consider “acceptable”.

How is El Niño likely to impact European gas and power prices in the coming months?

Andreas Schroeder: The World Meteorological Organization confirmed that it expects an El Niño summer, that is above normal temperatures in the Pacific Ocean with a high likelihood of 80-90%. There are indications that this year’s El Niño could be extremely strong.

High temperatures in East Asia during the summer can boost power demand for air conditioning, increasing competition for spot LNG cargoes just when Europe needs to step up its imports for storage injections.

China’s LNG demand looks stronger in June and, over the summer, will in part be driven by short-term requirements for peak gas-fired power generation during the summer heat. With India also buying LNG heavily, LNG into Europe could be challenged strongly until late in the third quarter and with the ICIS East Asia Index trading at a premium to the TTF.

On the other side, El Niño years may have reduced US hurricane risk which would be beneficial for US LNG production.

For the European winter it can be a wetter, warmer start, but a colder, drier first quarter. Overall, we have estimated an increase of 70 TWh winter gas demand in West & Central Europe[1] is possible in al El Niño year.

Matthew Jones: The traditional pattern you would expect with a strong El Niño would be bullish Q3 26 and Q1 27, and bearish Q4 26 (primarily due to the impact on temperatures and therefore demand). However, we modelled the three previous strong El Niño weather patterns that Europe has seen in the past 30 years, and they led to inconsistent price results. El Niño shifts your probabilities slightly, but it is not deterministic, so there is still a great deal of uncertainty over what impact we can expect on power market fundamentals and prices.

What are your price expectations for the second half of the year?

Andreas Schroeder: In the absence of conflict resolution in Iran, the missing Qatari LNG supply drives up price pressure. We are therefore bullish on summer gas prices until a peace deal is enforced. The Middle East supply crunch comes in combination with El Niño-induced strong Asian LNG demand and Europe’s need to refill storages. As storage injections are sluggish during early summer, we expect winter prices to gradually follow and move up as well. For 2027 and 2028, we see more signs of relaxation with new LNG supply coming online, but the timing and speed of project start will be critical as to easing the global balance.

Lewis Unstead: We expect EUAs to finish the year at around €80/t – broadly in line with current levels. We’ve seen compliance buying around €73-75/t provide support in recent weeks, while an estimated ~24Mt reduction in REPowerEU auction volumes in Jul-Aug and the lingering risk of delayed free allocation issuance should keep the market constructive into the September compliance window. However, a “political ceiling” around €90/t has become firmly entrenched, materially capping upside despite a tight market balance. With policy uncertainty likely to persist as ETS reform discussions progress through Parliament and Council into year-end, speculative participants are likely to remain very cautious.
